transcript.transcript: Well, welcome, Representative Santiago. Uh, my name is Charles Bennett, or Santiago. I am the director of the Puerto Rican Studies Initiative for Civic Engagement and Public Policy. Um, and we're really excited to have you here. Tell us a little bit about your family history and your connections to Puerto Rico, to Connecticut, to the United States. Uh, what were your parents dreams for you? What were your dreams as a resident in Connecticut? Well, first of all, thank you for having me here today. Uh, it is such an honor. And I'm so proud that you are really doing, uh, this series of histories of the Puerto Rican, uh, community in the state of Connecticut. Um, I am just, uh, being here for 50 years. Um, just starting getting used to being called a regular Connecticut resident because it takes a long time in the state of Connecticut for people to accept you. But, um, I'll talk a little bit about myself and how I got here. Um, I came to, um, I was born in Puerto Rico, in Naranjito, Puerto Rico. Um, in 1955. I came here when I was a year old in 1956. My brother was about six months old. Uh, when he when he when we came here. And my father, who was in the army, he was a soldier. He was in the Korean War, had just gotten out of the Korean War. And, um, him and my mother decided to come here, um, for a better life, uh, to, uh, because of economics and also, uh, looking for a job, um, after he got out of the service, and, uh, my mother followed him. I mean, because that's what women do in in our Puerto Rican community, we follow the men. And we came to New York City in 1956, and my father, uh, found a job in, uh, in on Wall Street, and he b worked in the garment industry, uh, for a very long time, until we moved to Connecticut in 1972. And, um, we were so, um, we came because we also had family in New York City. My mother had a sister that had bought a house in Brooklyn, and everybody from Puerto Rico. Um, she was born in Guaynabo. That's where she's from. And my father's from that. I hate, though, how they met. I don't know. So, um, but my my mother is the youngest of 11 kids, and she already had sisters that were living here in New York City. So we followed them, and we lived with them for a while because she had a house. And then we, you know, we got our own apartment. Um, but my father and mother were always looking for a better place to live. So we moved around a lot in New York City from different, um, bad places, ghettos, uh, always looking for better place to live. So we lived all over New York City until we came to Connecticut in 1972. When we came to Connecticut in 1972, I was a high school student, and I was going into my 12th year, uh, my fourth year, uh, as a 12th grader in, in, in school. So to me, coming to Connecticut was a was a culture shock because in New York City, everybody's black or brown. Everybody. There's different languages. Everybody has an accent. And we all got along so well together because everybody was either an immigrant or a migrant. And we, um, we expected, you know, we got along. All of us grew up together. But when you come into Connecticut, into Meriden, where the population is mostly 95%, uh, non-Hispanic or white, it was a culture shock to me. So in my 12th grade in, in Meriden, I was one of a dozen Latinos, Puerto Ricans and other, you know, Ecuadorians and Colombians that were going and were in high school. Um, they weren't that many blacks either. So there was only a few of us, and we bonded because we were all Hispanics, Latinos. Um, and then when I went in, uh, I was planning on going to college. My father always wanted us to, to keep going on in education. Um, he was going to college before he came to, uh, to New York City when he was at Puerto Rico. And, um, he left all of that in order to come, uh, to New York for a better life. And he, um, education to him was very, very important. Um, so he always made sure that we would get up in the morning. Uh, while we were growing up, we had breakfast. We had coffee with our breakfast since elementary school. And, um, that was our tradition. And we came home and my mother, uh, my mother ended up having seven kids. Um, that's how big my family was. And, um, but we were always looking for a better place. So we came to visit our uncle in Meriden at one time, and she thought that would be a better place. So we ended up moving to Meriden in 1972. Like I stated again, it was a culture shock. Um, I was already planning on going to college, and when I came here, I was just disillusioned because there weren't a lot of us that were a lot of encouragement, um, especially in the high school. But I did get a school counselor that had seen my grades from New York City and said, look, you can go to college. So, um, we ended up filling out the application, and she helped me with that because I had nobody to help me. My my parents didn't know what a lot of English. They didn't know how to read English. So she was the one that helped me to apply for college. I went to Southern Connecticut State College at that time. Now it's a university. And again, I went through a culture shock because there were not a lot of people that looked like me, that spoke Spanish, and the few of us that were that spoke Spanish and were Puerto Rican. So we got to bond and we got to go through the four years of college together. Um, but it was in high school where I learned to be engaged in after school sports and where I was engaged in doing a lot of other things because my parents were so strict. The girls always stayed home and the boys could do whatever they wanted to do. So I stayed home, and my only outlet in, in going and doing different things was being in after school clubs, and that's how I got away with it and being involved. Um, and so when I went to southern, um, again, I got involved in local, uh, college politics. We formed a coalition of Latin American students with Yale, with um, uh, Gateway College. Now, that was there then, uh, with Albertus Magnus, with eastern, all the universities. We formed this coalition because, again, there weren't a lot of us. So we all knew each other and we did a lot of protesting. We did a lot of sit ins, um, because we wanted Puerto Rican history. We wanted women's history. And, and those were things that were important to us because we knew that eventually the, the Puerto Rican population in, in the state of Connecticut was growing. Um, so we formed those organizations back then. Um, and then going back to, um, after college, I ended up going back to Meriden and I, uh, went to, uh, I became a bilingual teacher because my trade is my vocation was being a teacher. That's what I always wanted to be, was a teacher. And I played a lot of school and teacher with my brothers and sisters because they were younger than me. Um, and then after I graduated college, I by then I'm already back in Meriden. Um, I was teaching for a little while before I got married. Um, and then I went in, uh, got into a really bad marriage, and that stopped things for, uh, about ten years before I was able to get out of that, uh, that relationship in order to start getting involved again. But one of the things that I really got involved with when I had my son, um, he, uh, special needs intellectually, uh, developmentally challenged, and I got involved in, uh, in Meriden, um, making sure that he got the services he needed. And when he was in first grade, they kept him back once. And then when he was supposed to go to second grade, after they kept them back once they wanted to keep him back again. And I said, but there's got to be something, you know, with my teaching background, there's he needs to get tested. And I really had to fight for him to get into special ed, um, by going to board meetings. A board of education meetings, the city council meetings. And during that time I met Miguel Cofer, who was the executive director of Casa Boricua at that time. And she helped me through that whole process. Um, so that's how I started to get more involved in Meriden, in the community. Um, which I call community engagement, uh, instead of civic engagement, because we tend to we tend to, um, understand community engagement, whether you're involved with the church, whether you're involved in the ptos after, you know, after your kids while they were in school, um, whether you're involved in, uh, going to City Hall, excuse me, going to City Hall and saying, well, uh, my taxes are wrong. My car taxes are wrong. Something's going on. Um, but I did a lot of that advocacy through the board of Ed, and people saw potential in me because I was always there. And after going to the meetings, they were afraid that I was going to run against them. So they started asking me questions. And, uh, because there was nobody that looked like me going to these meetings. And, um. And Magali, uh, Cofer always helped me in, in, in making speeches and talking about how I felt, uh, with my son going into special ed, and that's how he got into special ed, and that's how I started my advocacy. After a while, they asked me to be on the Democratic Town Committee, uh, to get involved, and, uh, and I did because Magali Kupfer, uh, Marcelino Perez, Jose Rivera, um, all these folks, Frank Collazo, they were all involved in the Democratic Town Committee. And to me, they were my idols because they had been involved in politics since the 50s and the 60s in the city of Meriden. Um, and they had been on the city council. And back then when the voter, uh, the voter box was one lever, you pull it down, we were able to get Latinos on the city council. So we had three of them. We had, uh, Jimmy Verona, Frank Collazo, and we had, uh, Marcelino Perez. They were on the city council back then, but it wasn't until 2005 that there had been no Latinos on the city council, no Puerto Ricans on the city council since the 60s. And that's what Magali and all these, uh, senior advocates, these trailblazers were fighting for in the city of Meriden. It's a fascinating story. How, how how did your generation, your gender, your education shape these experiences will shape your identity as you were framing yourself as a Puerto Rican leader in Meriden. My education and my gender, um, had a lot to forming who I am today because, again, the people that got involved in the city of Meriden were all men. You. There was only one woman that I know that ran for city council and she didn't make it. Um, but all the people on the city council and all the Latinos that were on the board of Ed were all men. There were no women. We only had one woman of color. That was in 1974. Was the last woman of color, African American that was on the city council. She was a teacher and she was only there for four years. And then after that there was nobody else. But I think that being involved in the community, um, going to events, going to press conferences, advocating at City council for issues that had to do with the Puerto Rican community. Again, Casa Boricua was the, um, the star of of of the community because when people came from Puerto Rico, that's where they would go for help. They helped them, um, get assistance. They helped them with paperwork to get Social Security. They helped them get housing. They helped them get jobs. Um, when you'll see that when you talk to other leaders, like, um, Hector Cardona SR, the father of US Secretary Miguel Cardona, um, you'll see that it all goes back to Casa Boriqua. That was the place where everybody went, um, and everybody knew about the place. You didn't have to know it. You. You didn't have to know by TV. You didn't know about, um, texting, um, social media. There was no social media back then, but people knew about Casa Borrego by word of mouth, where that you were in the church, involved in the church, whether you were going to the Spanish stores, the bodegas that were in in Meriden, people knew about Casa because Casa Boricua is such an outreach in the community, and they were always representing the the Puerto Rican that came into, um, into Meriden that didn't know English. So they did a lot of advocacy in education to make sure they hire minority Spanish speaking teachers. If there was a problem in the school system where the behavior or there was a problem with somebody being, uh, with the parents was always there, um, to help them through that process. So Magali did, uh, Magali cover her? Her, uh, middle name was Padilla, and her family had been there since the 40s. They were one of the first families that came into Meriden. Um, so she was a pillar of everything that happened in Meriden through Casa Boricua. And we also have, uh, Lucy Gonzalez, who also was one of the ones that started Casa Boricua back then, who was an advocate. Um, so being, uh, with a bachelor's degree and being a woman and being able to fight for, um, issues that were important to us as a Puerto Rican community was what we did. And I got on the board. I was on the board for many years. Um, and that's how I did a lot of advocacy, being chair of the board. Um, and with Magali, um, fighting for what we felt was a piece of the pie at that time. So this might sound obvious to us, but how do you continue to retain your community ties? I mean, you've described a history that brought you to it. What do you do? What are you doing right now to maintain those community ties with the city manager? How do I maintain ties today? Uh, by staying busy. You have to, um. Again. Uh, as an elected official, I go to a lot of events. I get invited to a lot of places, and I'm still sometimes the only Puerto Rican there sitting at the table. You're a state. Representative? As a state representative? When I was on the city council, uh, I was on the city council from 2005 to 2011, um, for almost seven years. I was also appointed on the city council back in 1996 and 97. Um, the first Puerto Rican woman on the city council, I was back. That was back in 1996. And I was appointed to to complete somebody else's, um, four year term. Uh, there had been no Puerto Rican or Latinas ever on the city council we have been able to get a couple on the board of Ed, but never on the city council. And the city council, as you know, is the one that makes the decisions and the resolutions and the laws for the city of Meredith. And they make sure that everybody is, you know, is is looked out for in the budget. So everybody, the department's got money. But our thing back then was fighting for people at City Hall to get hired that spoke Spanish. And during this time, during the late 90s and the early 2000, more and more Latinos, Puerto Ricans were coming into Meriden. Um, by by 2020, we had, um, probably close to 15, 17,000, uh, Latinos in Meriden in 2010. We probably had like 13,000. And then like in, in two in, in, in in 2000, we were probably close to maybe 10,000. So the population was already growing from the 80s, the 90s, the early 2000. And um, and the more people came into Meriden, the more you had to fight, which is, which is odd because everything should be already, you know, inclusive, everything should be diverse, but it's not. So we have to fight to get more Spanish speaking teachers in the board of Ed, more administration. We had to, um, uh, recommend that, uh, Miguel Cardona was the first, uh, diverse, uh, Puerto Rican in the, uh, Board of Ed administration. Um, as an assistant superintendent. And, and the goal was to eventually him to become the superintendent of Meriden. But then other things popped up where he became the commissioner of education for the state of Connecticut. Then now, as you know, he's the Under secretary. He's the secretary for education at the federal level. But we always have to fight for things in Maryland in order to get them and advocate. Um, we had to make sure that the social service agencies had bilingual people that were that they were servicing because the population of Maryland now in the school system is almost like 55% Latinos, not, you know, non, non, uh, non Spanish speakers. Um, so we have to make sure we have to keep making sure that they are aware the administration at the city and the administration at the board of Ed, you got to be in their ear all the time. Well, you hired somebody for the board of Ed that's now a dean of something that you make sure that that person was Latino because of the large Latino population we have in the school system. So you always have to be in their ear, and you always have to be picking up the phone and say, okay, how come this happened and why didn't this person get hired? Um, and that's what we do. That's what I do in the city of Meriden as a politician. Um, and that's what we do now that the city council is more diverse with African Americans and Latinos, that's what they're always doing, is making sure that our folks are getting served just like everybody else, and everybody is treated the same. This is what community engagement is all about. And like I said, I get involved. I get invited to a lot of events. Um, as the dean of the Meriden delegation now, um, I've been there for 12 years as a state representative. I get invited to a lot of stuff, as we all do. And, and I, I make the time because I want to I want people to see that I'm not a politician that is absent. I want to I want them to see that I'm a politician that is involved. If there's an opening for the farmers market, I'm there 830 in the morning. If there's an opening for an event that the Meriden Green, that has to do with the book bags, giving out book bags in August, I'm there as a as a representative, not only of as a politician and as a state rep that represents my community, but also as a Latina, as a woman, that people see that these kids are seeing me as a role model. They might not know what a state representative is, but when I'm up on the stage and I'm there with the mayor and other people, they know that I'm important and that's what they need to see. They need to see that we are role models and we want them to say one, you know, I can also be up there. I can also be up on that stage. And a lot of people think of me as a one of the most powerful Latinas in the city of Meriden. Do I feel like that? Yeah, maybe sometimes. But I don't have to say anything. All I have to do is be present at somewhere and people know who I am because I've been involved in politics for so long. Um, and, you know, I had a mentor also, besides Magali Kupfer, I also had a mentor, Chris Donovan, who was involved in politics for a very long time. He eventually became the speaker of the House, and this was an opportunity for me to run for his seat when he ran for Congress. I wanted to run for his seat. And it's not that I was thinking in my long term goal was to be a state representative. I always thought I was going to be a city councilor for a long time. Um, not to be the mayor because the mayor in Meriden has no power. The city council is where the power is, and that's where you do. That's where you do. Your advocacy is with the city council. But, um, I, you know, the the the vacancy came up. I saw the opportunity people other people wanted the seat. And I said, well, why not me? Why can't I do it? I, I've been involved in politics for a long time, and I, I might not be the greatest speaker. I might that might not have the best charisma. But I know what my community needs. I know what they feel like because people will stop me and stop and shop. They will stop me at the stores. They will stop me anywhere and say, hey, this is what's happening to me, can you help me? My thing is about helping my community, no matter what color you are, because I represent everybody in my district. But I want to be sure that people see that there is a Latina that's looking out for me. Can you talk a little bit about whether local organizations are effectively addressing the needs of Puerto Ricans in Meriden? Uh, and what makes them effective? Or you can give us an example. Yeah. One makes agencies in in Meriden effective. And are they being effective? I would say yes. And then I would say we still got some ways to go. I would say that we have organizations like Child Guidance Women and Family Center, um, the police cadets, all these organizations, the Boys and Girls Club, they are making sure that they're hiring Spanish speaking folks because we have we still have a very, uh, monolingual, uh, community in Meredith. Um, and we still have more and more people coming in right now. We're not only seeing Puerto Ricans coming in, but we're seeing, uh, uh, immigrants from Mexico, from Costa Rica, from Colombia, Ecuador that are also coming into, uh, Maryland. And so we're also fighting for them to make sure that they get the services. And, and a lot of the agencies in Meriden know that now that they need to speak, they I mean, they need to hire Spanish speaking staff. Uh, and that's something I'm always pushing at City Hall. Uh, like economic development. My district is in the middle of the city, so most of the bodegas and most of the stores in my district are owned by small business entrepreneurs that are Spanish speaking. They don't have the time to take a course during the day. Why? Because they're tending to their business. So you have to find a way to get them engaged and you have to go to them. That's how you do it. When we did the, uh, during the Covid, we went to them business by business, giving them test kits, giving them masks. I worked with the Spanish American Merchants Association from Hartford, who gave me the tools and said, look, take this into your community. So I did that, and that's how I know what's going on in the city of Meriden, because I do door to door and I do and I, and I visit business to business, anything that's coming into my district, I make sure that I know about it. I do my business in my district unless the store's not in my district, but I like to spend money in my district because you're helping the small businesses that are there. So as far as nonprofit organizations, as far as businesses, they're always making sure that they hire people that speak Spanish. We have an AutoZone. Most of the people in that AutoZone in the middle of my district are all Latinos that work there. So they attract people because now you have someone that you can talk to in Spanish that knows exactly what you want for your car, right. So, um, this is what's important when you are an elected official or if you're involved with your community activist, you really have to know your community, and you really have to be there for them when there are issues that come up. Uh. Lincoln has one of the highest concentrations of Puerto Ricans in the US. Uh, in your opinion, why did Puerto Ricans come to Connecticut or Maryland? Well, from what I know of the history of Meriden, you have a family like the Cardona's and the Gonzalez's and the Tauruses that all living Aguada. So they all come into, you know, family brings more family and they say, oh, you got to come down. I got a job for you or you got to come down. It's better here because the, the, the, the, the quality of life is better and education is better. And your kids will grow up and you know, and get better jobs. And that's what it's all about. So when you have everybody from Aguada and Kong and Aguadilla all coming in to Meredith, that's where usually everybody's from. It's the same thing. When you go to New Haven, everybody's from the same area. You go to Hartford. People are from Brownsville, Caguas, um, and Meriden. It's Aguada. So I'm not from Aguada. I'm. I'm like the stepchild that came in from that Agito. Uh, and and and, you know, and I was raised in New York City, but I, you know, I spent my adult life in Connecticut. Um, but Connecticut, I think, has a quality, quality, quality of life that people are looking for, that it's a little bit more slower. Um, it's more, um, you take your time and doing things. The education is great. Not to say that New York City wasn't like that in New York City. The education back at that time, back in the late 60s and 70s when I was in school, was really the education was excellent. It was so good that by the time I came for 12th grade, I had done all my requirements, and for my 12th grade, I ended up taking five history courses to make up for the courses that I couldn't take. Um, because I had finished all my requirements by the time I was a junior in high school in New York City. Um, but it was, I guess, the, the, the environment of of fast paced New York City. Um, things were changing back then, and coming to Connecticut was a better life for us. Like, I believe it was a better life for a lot of the Puerto Ricans that came from the same section of Puerto Rico. Um, and family follows family and friends and, and they all help each other because they stay at each other's houses until you get an apartment. Back then, everything was a lot cheaper. The housing was a lot cheaper, less expensive, but now it's a little bit more expensive. But, um, I think that you know it. Connecticut is attractive because of the schools, uh, because of better housing. Um, you know, better education and things are a lot. You have more trees, more backyards, you know, and that's something you don't get a lot in New York City. Um, on that. Note, what does it mean for you to raise a family here in Connecticut? What does it mean to raise a family? I think that this was the best place, especially for me to raise my own kids. Um, in the city of Meriden. Um, I wouldn't have wanted them to be raised in New York City. Um, sometimes when you have, like, my parents had seven kids and the girls never went out anywhere. Um, my parents weren't because they didn't speak English. My father was okay with English because he worked in the factories, but he he worked in a factory all his life. And, um, starting at $1.35 an hour. And by the time he left in the 70s, um, he was probably making, like, 4 or $5 an hour. Um, he when he when when we came to Connecticut, um, my father, we none of us drove. Uh, my father didn't drive. He only drove when he was in the army. Uh, we didn't nobody had a license. Because in New York City, you don't need to drive. You have busing and you have trains, that's all. That's how we all traveled. But when you come to Connecticut, you have to have a car. Um, and I know that the the idea is to move away from cars and transit, but you still need a car. Um, my father walked from Meriden to Wallingford, which is probably close to 15 or 20 miles every day for a year and a half to go to work, because that's the only place where he found a job. And until he found someone that that he knew in the factory international silver at that time, that they, um, they gave him a ride. And it wasn't until about, uh, two years to three years that he bought a station wagon. Um, but my father used to walk to work all the time, and I had to walk to school. Uh, when I was in, in, um, when I was in 12th grade in New York City, we took the train. That was the transportation. But the the quality of life. It's it's one of the best places to raise kids. Uh, because the quality of life is so much higher. Uh, you get paid more for jobs. I think you have more opportunities for young people to to come here and, uh, buy a home. There's programs. As a legislator, we always coming up with ideas and how to attract the young people to to come to Connecticut, but also to stay in Connecticut. So, uh, we're still there's still a lot of other stuff we still have to work for because the cost of living is is higher in Connecticut. But what I find that when you buy a gallon of milk in Connecticut, that that gallon of milk is going to cost the same as it does in Virginia or North Carolina or Texas or Florida. I have friends that have moved to Florida because they wanted a slower pace. They didn't want the winter. All this other stuff, they took a big cut in pay working for the state, but they couldn't make it because the, the, the cost of living is just about the same as if you're getting paid less. So she ended up a friend of mine. She ended up moving back to Connecticut and going back to work to state government. Because what she was getting there 10 to $15,000 less, she couldn't make it. So, um, you know, everybody's got complaints about Connecticut. Um, but I think this is one of the best places to live. The the the the weather is better. You don't get a lot of tornadoes. You don't get hurricanes, you don't get you know, you get your winters. And winters have been mild the last few years. But, um, I think the, the, the, the, the quality of life is the health care is better in the state of Connecticut. People from Florida and Texas and the South and up coming back for better health care in the state of Connecticut where like the three, the third the highest in health care in the country. Um, so those are things that we have to, of course, pay for. Um, and people get paid a lot better in Connecticut, but of course, everything's a little bit more expensive. The taxes. Yeah, they do kill you a little bit, but you're not going to find a better quality of life anywhere else, I think, and anywhere else. You know, my daughter lived in Montana for 16 years, and I used to go visit there every Thanksgiving and, um, even even things that I felt when I first moved to Connecticut, I felt when I got when I went there, when you walk into a restaurant, it's, you know, everybody just looks at you because they don't know what Puerto Ricans are. My daughter was the only Puerto Rican in Billings, Montana. They thought she was either Mexican or she was Native American because she, you know, she looked like she's Native American, but, um, she, um, she, you know, she also had a hard time in Montana because she wasn't accepted all the time. Um, but she loved it there because it's a lot slower than Connecticut. She still loves it. Um, but it's, you know, Connecticut. Um, to me, I love Connecticut. It's probably the only, uh, place that I've been stable living for 50 years in Meriden. Where in New York City, we. Every year, every six months, we move from place to place to place, always looking for a better place to live. Um, a place that wasn't, uh, in the ghetto. A place that was better housing. A place that wasn't infested by. By drug addicts back then. Um, and Connecticut to us was like, you know, it was like, uh, the best place to live when we first moved here. When you think about challenges that you face when pursuing community engagement projects, can you identify some of those challenges that that people face or that you face, uh, when you're trying to do community engagement? Some of the challenges, um, I don't have a lot of challenges now, I guess, in Meriden because people know who I am. But before it was a lot. It was very challenging, um, getting involved in the community because people didn't accept me. Um, because I wasn't a, uh, a Cardona. Okay. I wasn't, uh, a teacher at that time. Um, I got more involved into social services. Um, especially with Casa Boricua. Um, so people I wasn't that active in, I mean, vocal. Um, I just listened a lot and I wrote down a lot of notes. And then when I had to speak, that's when I spoke. So people got to respect me more for that. But as I got more in politics, people got to respect me more. Um, because then I had this title and this power that they said, oh, we gotta, you know, say Hilda Santiago. Everybody knows Hilda, Santiago and Meredith. Um, but it took me a long time to get there. Because if you don't like anywhere else that you go, if you don't have a title or if you're, um, not a doctor, um, if you're not a teacher, if you're not a politician, or if you don't work in City hall, people really don't pay attention to you. It's when you start getting more involved in the community, um, with the churches, with, uh, with the after school programs, um, getting more involved in volunteering in boards and commissions. Um, that was another thing. A lot of the boards and commissions don't have people that look like me. You know, a single head of household mom, a Latina, and, uh, and then trying to, um, uh, get involved in politics. It took me to leave Meriden for a while, uh, because I couldn't get hired anywhere because I was so involved in politics. It took me to get out of Meriden and get another job in New Haven and then come back, uh, to be more respected, uh, because then people see all the other stuff that you're doing, especially with housing and education and health care. Uh, those are the fields that I was involved in when I left Meriden, working in Meriden. And I went to other towns to work. I worked in in Waterbury for a while, um, and became an assistant director for the Head Start program. Um, because they my experiences, I worked in the Head Start program, for example. So I was the educational coordinator, uh, for a few years. I worked there for almost eight years in the 80s and the 90s. And then I was the interim director, so they didn't want to give me the job as the director because I didn't have a master's degree. But I had the experience and the teachers all worked with me. They respected me. I worked with the community. I worked with foundations. Uh, we got grant money in. I worked with the grants people from Boston. But because I didn't have a master's degree, I wasn't hired as the director. I was good as an interim director, but not as a director. And so I said, okay, so they hired somebody else. And then I had to sit back. I said, well, if he's a director, then he needs to be responsible for all the stuff that I've been doing and I'm just going to do my part. Uh, the, the agency that had Head Start lost the program, and again, the YWCA took over the program. Again, I applied for the director's job. I didn't get the director's job. They want to hire me as an assistant director, but give the job to a woman from Wallingford that had a master's degree, and they felt that she was more qualified. And I said, okay. And when they offered me the job for assistant director, I said, no, because what's going to happen is, is I end up doing all the work because I've already built those relationships. I do all the work, and then the director gets all the credit and then I don't. And so but I'm good enough to do all the behind the scenes work to lift up this Head Start program. But I'm not good enough to run the program because I don't have a master's degree. Those are the challenges that I had. That's why I had to leave Meriden and work in other areas, because there wasn't that respect that that I should have been getting as a good community person, engaged in the community. I have people, you know, teachers and teachers, aides and coordinators that were working with me. I had foundations that were working with me. The the grantee was working with me, but I wasn't good enough to be a director. So those I mean, I should write a book. I mean, of all these challenges I had and then people didn't, didn't, um, respect you for that because you weren't a director of a program. So I had to leave, and that's how I, I gained my experience at other in other arenas. And I got to know more people around the state. If you were to compare the types of community engagement in the 70s to today. What comes to mind. If I was to compare between the 70s and now? Um, I would say that it was a lot more difficult back then. Um, because there weren't enough of us. And, um, I'm surprised I'm not burnt out, but there weren't enough of us. And then to get people involved, it's that they're always scared about getting involved in politics or getting involved in the community, or being on a board of child guidance, or being on a Human Services board at City Hall. Uh, people just went around their lives and let other people do it for them and make those decisions. And but they never wanted to make those decisions. Um, so now you have more of a younger generation that really wants to get involved. And I see that's what the difference is. So it's always important for me, uh, to mentor someone that would want to take my place eventually. Um, and I've worked with several people on the city council, Um, and I still have to find someone now, uh, for for when I leave. Um, because I'm not going to do this forever, but my but my interest is to mentor someone, or not even one person. A few young people. Um, mostly women. Um, because women don't get involved in politics as they should. They'll get involved in health care, they'll get involved in housing, they'll get involved in small businesses. They'll become members of the chamber. Um, they'll do that, all that other stuff. But they stay away from politics. And if you're if. And then they talk about, well, let's do policy change. How are you going to do policy policy change if you don't get involved, if you don't have a voice at the table, it doesn't mean that you have to be an elected official, but you can be the person behind a candidate that helps if you have people that, um, when you're registering people, especially when you're registering people to vote. I do a lot of door knocking and I register people to vote. It's not just about registering them to vote, but you have to go back and say, look, we have an election. I'll take you out and let's go vote. I'll show you how it's done because people get intimidated by the system. I don't know what it is, but when when you're in Puerto Rico, everybody votes. That's a holiday for everybody, right? I mean, I lived in Puerto Rico for two years in middle school, and, and and when it came to the elections, everybody was involved. Everything closed down. It was a big party. People really took it seriously and went out to vote. When they come here, I don't know what happens. I don't know if they get intimidated by the English language. I don't know if they get intimidated because they don't understand what's going on. And that's why we have to hold people that are in elected office accountable to make sure that if their population that they want to represent everything, should be in English and Spanish. When you go to places, make sure that you say, oh, la Como estan? Say, because then they know that you know the language and they feel comfortable and they can trust you because it's all about trust. Um, but women don't tend to get involved in politics and I don't I don't know what it is. It's, you know, I'll go to an event of 3 or 400 Latino women that are small business owners that are really, um, getting this certificate, that certificate really improving themselves. But when it comes to politics, oh, no, I don't want to be a politician. There's nothing wrong with being a politician, because as long as you hold on to your morals and your values and that you represent your community and and you get other people to follow you, then, uh, and you also help them out, then it's there's nothing wrong with being a politician because at least I am there to voice what they but what they can't voice themselves. Or they might have trouble voicing. And, um, you have to have a seat at the table, because if you don't have people that look like me, then your vote is for what? Because you're not really engaged. You're not engaged with the voter. You're not engaged with the politician, you're not engaged with your community. You're letting people do whatever they want with you. I met a woman, for example, who's very involved in this other town. I won't mention it. And she was born and raised there, but she bought a house in Meredith. So I said, oh, you do all this stuff for this gentleman that is running for mayor. I have never seen you in Meriden doing anything for Meredith. And she says, oh, because I know people in Bristol. But I said, but you have a house in Meriden, so people on the city council are making decisions for you, and you own a home in Meriden and your taxes are going up. So what are you saying about that? What voice do you have? And she said to me, oh, I never realized that. That's what she said to me. I'm going to get involved right after the elections. I says, okay, I'm going to hold you to that, and I'll go back and, you know, seek her out and said, look, let's get involved in Meredith, because maybe she might be the one that might follow in my footsteps. Um, because if she's so involved in that other town and she's doing great work there, do it in your own town where you are paying taxes and your kids are going to school. What voice are you? What voice are you giving your community? If you're not involved in your own community and your own backyard? So she kind of said, yeah, that kind of makes sense. So and I said it in a nice way so that she doesn't feel like, you know, defensive because you have to be so careful that people don't get defensive. And, um, and that's what that's what I'm all about is getting folks engaged, making them feel comfortable that all politicians are just like us. Just. They're like regular people. You can't put them on a pedestal, because if you keep putting other politicians on a pedestal, that's where they're always going to stay, and then they're going to do whatever they want with you, right? And they can make decisions for you, um, whichever way you want. And then you have nothing to say. But if you don't get involved, then don't complain when decisions are being made that you don't like. If I were to if you were to tell me about a signature project that you've taken a signature accomplishment, can you describe what made it special? I think what what would seem to make a project that I've really got involved in a signature project is making sure that I brought back money for youth services. Um, I brought back money through the state legislature, um, for programs, for after school programs, because most of the children that are going to these after school programs are blacks and Latinos. We have a very large Latino population in Meriden. Our population in Meriden for African Americans might be like 5 or 6000. We got maybe 17,000 already. Latinos in Meriden, very mixed, um, cultures of of Latinos. Um, and we need after school programs for them to keep them out of, out of the streets, creating problems in the streets. So keeping them engaged, whether it's boxing, whether it's, um, music, dancing, uh, get them involved in, um, doing things like, um, shooting arrows, um, going canoeing, things that, that I was never brought up with. Right, that I never experienced until I was an adult. An adult. But these are things that we have to engage our kids in so that they have they understand of all this variety of, of of of of sports or activities or music or dancing that is out there, that the world is open for them if they want to be a musician, if they want to be a dancer, if they want to play basketball, everything's not about sports. There's other things, right? Uh, if they want to sing, um, in after school programs is what gets them involved. So I make sure that I bring in money to the after school programs. There's ten of them in Meriden that I. 11 of them that I deal with. And also, uh, a peer advocacy group that has deals with autism. Um, I'm really involved in a lot of, you know, um, issues with special ed. Um, so I that's something that I, uh, think is, uh, probably will be my legacy is bringing in money for the youth. When you think about Puerto Rican politics in Connecticut, where do you see, uh, Puerto Ricans going in the next decade or so? We just started a Latino caucus, a Puerto Rican, Connecticut, Puerto Rican, and Latino caucus. Um, with the legislators, which is very, uh, mix of Latino, different Latino, uh, groups involved. Um, but I believe that we, um, we still have a long way to go. Um, we still need more, for example, more judges, um, trying to convince, uh, Puerto Ricans or Latinos to become judges is like, uh, pulling a tooth because they make so much money working in the private sector. Why would they want to make money as a judge? But sometimes I when I talk to them, I says, well, you're doing so you're doing much more for the community as a whole When you're there as a representative of that community, because you understand it more. I hope you understand it more than anybody else would. Um, but we still have a long way to go. And the the, the Puerto Ricans slash Latinos, uh, have a lot more to do. We just, um, for example, just passed a law to make sure that in the school system everything was in English and Spanish, and we passed that law because in Meriden we do that. Everything is in English and Spanish. The, the, the Board of Ed administration is very, um, cognizant of the population in the city of Meriden that everything has to go home in English and Spanish. They have to make sure we have parent advocates that speak Spanish. But when I go when I go to the state legislature, it's not happening in Bridgeport. It's not happening in Torrington, it's not happening maybe in Danbury that has like 52 languages. But, um, so we have to, you know, pass a law so that all 169 towns have whatever population you have in your school system, that everything is done in English and Spanish. Those are little things that sometimes I don't realize that we're already doing. In Meriden, for example. Another example is, um, as with my advocacy and magalis advocacy, we in our precincts and our voting precincts, we made sure that we have someone that speaks Spanish in every precinct. 30 years ago, that wasn't happening. But as we got involved in the 80s and 90s and saw the need for it, we make sure that we go out and we get the people because they want us to get the people, they don't want to do the work. We end up doing the work to get people that are pulled, that work in the polls, that at least one person in each precinct speaks Spanish, because we don't want that person that comes in and looks at the, at the, at the poll workers, and they don't look like them, and they're afraid to ask a question because that's what'll happen. They go in, they don't see anybody that speaks Spanish. They walk out and we don't want that. We want to make sure that our people are voting. Um, we have to make sure that, um, the, the, the, the folks, uh, see what we went through. And Puerto Ricans now will keep carrying that torch in the future. We have a Connecticut Hispanic Democratic Caucus we've had for about 16, 17 years and made up of politicians, small business people, community activists, regular people. We just now, um, voted on, uh, on. Kay Munoz, who is a 27 year old young Peruvian afro-latina who is now took over the Connecticut Hispanic Democratic Caucus. So that's what our job is, is to mentor these young folks, because we're not going to be around forever, like I said. And the population in Connecticut is growing more and more Hispanic, where in another ten, 15 years we're not going to be the minority anymore. We're going to be the majority. And so we have to make sure that things are in place for them in housing and education and health care, in jobs, manufacturing jobs, that we all have a piece of that pie just like everybody else does. And we want to make sure that our people are treated the same. Um, because if we don't fight for that, they're going to be treated, they're going to be treated with discrimination. They're not going to be included. Um, the diversity is not going to be there. And, and, um, like, I have one of my colleagues who was a former state rep, she said I had the opportunity. She's she's, you know, uh, she's white. She said I had the opportunity to move out of Meriden. Um, but I didn't move out of Meriden because the schools are so diverse. My children grew up with kids of other nations, of other countries, of other languages. And sometimes you're not going to get that in Cheshire. You might not get that in prospect, you might not get that in, in Derby. Um, and and in Meriden, we are like a melting pot. So our children are getting all that kind of experience. But we see that we need to still keep building our Latino base. Um, because if we don't keep building that Latino base, it's going to disappear. And we have to make sure that we have more Latinos that get elected to the legislature. Right now, there's only three Latinas. I'm the first Latina on the city council, first Puerto Rican woman, the first Puerto Rican state representative, um, from Meriden. As a Latina. So I'm still doing first, hopefully in another 15, 20 years. We don't have to do that anymore because it'll be something that'll come natural. Um, and people won't even think about, um, whether you're the first Puerto Rican or the first black, you know, it's it's something that will be so inclusive that we don't even have to think that way. Hopefully. Let me ask you one last question, and you start to allude to this when you're talking about the Puerto Rican and Latino Caucus. Uh, what do you see the role of coalition politics with other Latinos, with African Americans, with other, uh, groups or ethnic groups or races in the state? What do we see as collaboratively, I. The role of coalition building, the. Coalition building. Um, I see more of it going on. Uh, we're building, uh, more coalitions, um, and more collaborative, more inclusiveness and more diversity because we're showing the, um, with the Connecticut and Latino Caucus, we can be part of any caucus. We can be part of the Black and Puerto Rican caucus, which we are. There's 40 of us in the legislature together, but we wanted to do our own Connecticut, Puerto Rican and Latino Caucus because we have issues as far as culture, language, um, getting involved. We have a lot, uh, fewer we have more issues that are different than the Black and Puerto Rican caucus. Um, but we can work together to fight for one cause, for example, with juvenile justice, which we just did, um, this past session, uh, two years ago when we did the police accountability bill, we worked together in coalitions not only with the Black and Puerto Rican caucus. And back then we didn't have the Latino caucus, but we worked with the moderates. We worked with the progressives in order to build coalitions in order to pass a bill. Because if you don't do that at the at the General Assembly, you won't get your bills passed. So you have to network, you have to, um, engage. You have to make sure that you're at the table. Um, because that's one thing I do. I like to make sure that there's if you if you're having a panel, for example, if there's a panel going on at the legislature and you have a black and you have a white, um, and you have a few more blacks and a few more whites, where's the Latino? And so now they're so used to me asking that question that they made sure that I now I don't have to ask it anymore. They make sure that there's a Latino on that panel that is also dealing with health care, with housing, with all other issues, reproductive rights, because that affects our our community in a little bit of a different way. But we're still going through those same issues. Let's build these coalitions and work together. And don't forget that the again, and we always have to throw it at a lot of people's faces that the Latino, Hispanic, Puerto Rican, whatever you want to call it, population is growing in the state of Connecticut and you need to include us. We can't be we can't be behind you anymore. We have to have a seat at the table and we have to have a voice. So that's what our job is, is to make sure that they're cognizant that we're out there. And because we have this power, of course, as a legislator, that there's a lot that we can do, but let's do it all together. Let's not do it separately. So that's why it's important to build those coalitions and to keep it going. Thank you. This is really helpful. Thank you. Thank you for interesting. Thank you for having me. I just wish we had more time, but, um, there'll be other conversations. We will. Thank you so much.

lines.line: # Introduction to Representative Santiago

> The speaker expresses gratitude for being invited to share their story and is proud of the series highlighting the Puerto Rican community in Connecticut. The speaker acknowledges the long process of being accepted as a Connecticut resident and is poised to discuss their personal history and connection to Puerto Rico, Connecticut, and the United States.

metadata.title: Introduction to Representative Santiago
metadata.synopsis: The speaker expresses gratitude for being invited to share their story and is proud of the series highlighting the Puerto Rican community in Connecticut. The speaker acknowledges the long process of being accepted as a Connecticut resident and is poised to discuss their personal history and connection to Puerto Rico, Connecticut, and the United States.

lines.line: # Early Life and Immigration to Connecticut

> The speaker was born in Puerto Rico and moved to New York City at a young age. Their father, a Korean War veteran, sought better economic opportunities after his military service. The family's journey included a move to Connecticut in 1972, where the speaker experienced a cultural shock due to the lack of diversity compared to New York City.

metadata.title: Early Life and Immigration to Connecticut
metadata.synopsis: The speaker was born in Puerto Rico and moved to New York City at a young age. Their father, a Korean War veteran, sought better economic opportunities after his military service. The family's journey included a move to Connecticut in 1972, where the speaker experienced a cultural shock due to the lack of diversity compared to New York City.

lines.line: # Educational Journey and Early Community Engagement

> During the speaker's final year of high school in Connecticut, they faced a cultural shock but found solace in bonding with other Latino students. The speaker's father valued education, which led to the speaker attending Southern Connecticut State College. The speaker's involvement in college politics and advocacy for Puerto Rican history and women's history was a formative experience.

metadata.title: Educational Journey and Early Community Engagement
metadata.synopsis: During the speaker's final year of high school in Connecticut, they faced a cultural shock but found solace in bonding with other Latino students. The speaker's father valued education, which led to the speaker attending Southern Connecticut State College. The speaker's involvement in college politics and advocacy for Puerto Rican history and women's history was a formative experience.

lines.line: # Political Involvement and Advocacy

> The speaker's political involvement was shaped by their education and gender, as they were often the only woman in political spaces. They were inspired by local leaders and became the first Puerto Rican woman on the city council in Meriden. The speaker's advocacy focused on education, special needs services, and representation within the community.

metadata.title: Political Involvement and Advocacy
metadata.synopsis: The speaker's political involvement was shaped by their education and gender, as they were often the only woman in political spaces. They were inspired by local leaders and became the first Puerto Rican woman on the city council in Meriden. The speaker's advocacy focused on education, special needs services, and representation within the community.

lines.line: # Maintaining Community Ties and Representation

> The speaker maintains community ties by staying active and visible in various events as a state representative. They emphasize the importance of being a role model, especially for the Latino community, and strive to ensure that their constituents are well-represented and served.

metadata.title: Maintaining Community Ties and Representation
metadata.synopsis: The speaker maintains community ties by staying active and visible in various events as a state representative. They emphasize the importance of being a role model, especially for the Latino community, and strive to ensure that their constituents are well-represented and served.

lines.line: # Local Organizations and Community Needs

> The speaker believes that local organizations in Meriden are generally effective in addressing the needs of Puerto Ricans, but there is still room for improvement. They highlight the importance of hiring Spanish-speaking staff and engaging with the community, especially in providing services to a diverse and growing Latino population.

metadata.title: Local Organizations and Community Needs
metadata.synopsis: The speaker believes that local organizations in Meriden are generally effective in addressing the needs of Puerto Ricans, but there is still room for improvement. They highlight the importance of hiring Spanish-speaking staff and engaging with the community, especially in providing services to a diverse and growing Latino population.

lines.line: # Puerto Rican Migration to Connecticut

> The speaker explains that family connections and the promise of a better quality of life and education are the main reasons Puerto Ricans migrate to Connecticut. They discuss the historical migration patterns and the attraction of Connecticut's slower pace and opportunities compared to New York City.

metadata.title: Puerto Rican Migration to Connecticut
metadata.synopsis: The speaker explains that family connections and the promise of a better quality of life and education are the main reasons Puerto Ricans migrate to Connecticut. They discuss the historical migration patterns and the attraction of Connecticut's slower pace and opportunities compared to New York City.

lines.line: # Raising a Family in Connecticut

> The speaker reflects on the benefits of raising a family in Connecticut, citing better job opportunities, education, and quality of life. Despite higher living costs, the speaker believes Connecticut offers a superior environment for family life compared to other states.

metadata.title: Raising a Family in Connecticut
metadata.synopsis: The speaker reflects on the benefits of raising a family in Connecticut, citing better job opportunities, education, and quality of life. Despite higher living costs, the speaker believes Connecticut offers a superior environment for family life compared to other states.

lines.line: # Challenges in Community Engagement

> The speaker discusses the challenges they faced in community engagement, including gaining respect and overcoming barriers due to not fitting certain expected roles or titles. They emphasize the importance of being involved in the community and the need to mentor future leaders.

metadata.title: Challenges in Community Engagement
metadata.synopsis: The speaker discusses the challenges they faced in community engagement, including gaining respect and overcoming barriers due to not fitting certain expected roles or titles. They emphasize the importance of being involved in the community and the need to mentor future leaders.

lines.line: # Evolution of Community Engagement

> The speaker compares community engagement from the 70s to today, noting that it was more difficult in the past due to fewer people being involved. They stress the importance of mentoring the younger generation to ensure continued engagement and representation in politics and community activism.

metadata.title: Evolution of Community Engagement
metadata.synopsis: The speaker compares community engagement from the 70s to today, noting that it was more difficult in the past due to fewer people being involved. They stress the importance of mentoring the younger generation to ensure continued engagement and representation in politics and community activism.

lines.line: # Future of Puerto Rican Politics in Connecticut

> The speaker is optimistic about the future of Puerto Rican politics in Connecticut, highlighting the formation of the Connecticut Puerto Rican and Latino Caucus. They emphasize the need for continued advocacy for representation in various sectors, including the judiciary, and the importance of mentoring young leaders.

metadata.title: Future of Puerto Rican Politics in Connecticut
metadata.synopsis: The speaker is optimistic about the future of Puerto Rican politics in Connecticut, highlighting the formation of the Connecticut Puerto Rican and Latino Caucus. They emphasize the need for continued advocacy for representation in various sectors, including the judiciary, and the importance of mentoring young leaders.

lines.line: # Coalition Politics and Inclusivity

> The speaker discusses the importance of coalition politics and working collaboratively with other ethnic groups to achieve common goals. They highlight the need for inclusivity and ensuring that the growing Latino population has a voice in decision-making processes at all levels.

metadata.title: Coalition Politics and Inclusivity
metadata.synopsis: The speaker discusses the importance of coalition politics and working collaboratively with other ethnic groups to achieve common goals. They highlight the need for inclusivity and ensuring that the growing Latino population has a voice in decision-making processes at all levels.

story.title: Hilda Santiago
story.description: The transcript is an in-depth interview with Representative Santiago, exploring her journey from Puerto Rico to Connecticut, and her extensive involvement in community and political advocacy. Santiago discusses her family's migration for a better life, her educational challenges, and the cultural shock she faced. She highlights her advocacy work in education, focusing on bilingual representation and services for special needs children. Santiago's political career includes significant efforts to increase Latino political representation and foster community engagement. She also stresses the importance of coalition building among diverse ethnic groups to address collective issues in the state.
